events.text:
# Benchmark level assignment

Build a complete level according to `docs/level-brief.md`. Read `AGENTS.md` and `docs/level-authoring.md` as directed there. All repository instructions and the standing brief apply.

## Level identity

- Level id: `mass-driver-wo4m`
- Display title: `Mass Driver`

Use this identity consistently in the level directory, registry entry, metadata, and generated gallery card. The level directory must be exactly `src/levels/mass-driver-wo4m/`; do not use a shortened module-folder name.

## Benchmark additions

Aim for a **60-second playable run**. A duration from **55 to 65 seconds** is acceptable when needed to end on a natural musical phrase. This covers active gameplay after START and before the run summary; attract mode and REPLAY are outside it.

Demonstrate your attention to detail and creativity through this work. The expected standard is a polished showcase level, not merely a gate-passing implementation.

## Assigned theme

# Mass Driver

Build a level where you're riding a payload down a huge orbital railgun. The whole thing is basically a tunnel of glowing accelerator rings and you pass through one ring exactly on every beat, so the speed and the music are the same thing. As the run goes on the rings get spaced further apart (you're accelerating) but you still hit them on the beat, and they glow hotter as you go faster. Hot here means electric, not fire: arc blue through violet toward blinding white. The gun is also the instrument - a bass hum that climbs in pitch across the whole run, under a locked, hypnotic pulse.

Enemies are defense drones threading between the coils.

Boss: the gun's safety mechanisms are jammed and the final firing charge is already building. You can hear it - rising hum, rings burning hotter, the whole tunnel ramping up. Blow up the jammed safety interlocks before the charge peaks. Clear them all in time and the gun fires, launching you out of the muzzle into open space at insane speed, everything goes quiet, level ends. Too slow and the charge has nowhere to go - the barrel blows with you in it.



# Delegation protocol

Your work will be evaluated on a quality/cost pareto curve. Therefore you are encouraged to use your built in support for delegating work to subagents running cheaper models.

Delegate to model: `opus` with reasoning level `high`

You remain fully responsible for the quality of the final product. Take an active role in refining the outputs from the subagent, not just passive review.
I'll start by reading the brief and authoring docs, then survey the existing levels to understand the conventions.

4	
5	## Assignment
6	
7	Every level is the same game at its core: the player flies forward automatically along a rail through your world, holds to charge, sweeps the reticle across up to six targets, and releases to fire the whole volley — Rez is the common ancestor. Runs are short and meant to be replayed. The theme supplies the world, mood, and arc this happens in; it does not restate these mechanics.
8	
9	Build a complete level under `src/levels/<id>/` and register it in `src/levels/index.ts`. Within the shared lock-on mechanics you own everything: the rail, the spawn choreography, the enemy kinds and their motion, scoring, the entire visual language (environment, enemies, letter glyphs, effects), and the entire soundtrack and sound design. All of it procedural.
10	
11	Start from `npm run scaffold -- --id <id>`. Then read `docs/level-authoring.md`, `src/engine/lock-on-runner-types.ts`, and `docs/level-gallery.md`. For quality calibration, read the spine of the level the gallery identifies as the current bar. Full-source reading of other levels is optional depth, not a requirement.
12	
13	## Theme
14	
15	_Filled in per assignment. Direct the level's world, visual language, dramatic arc, musical character, and signature player-facing moments; do not repeat the shared rail-shooter contract or prescribe source-level implementation._
16	
17	## Effort
18	
19	This is a showcase piece, not a prototype or a proof of concept. Polish is the point: a visitor should play your level for thirty seconds and want to play it again. Budget real time for iteration on feel — enemy motion, effect timing, and the mix are where levels are won, not in the first working version.
20

## Summary

I built the full procedural soundtrack + sound design for Mass Driver in two files I own exclusively:
- `/tmp/raild-run-eztdwig5twea/src/levels/mass-driver-wo4m/audio.ts` (spine: score, arrangement, player instruments, event handlers)
- `/tmp/raild-run-eztdwig5twea/src/levels/mass-driver-wo4m/audio-voices.ts` (leaves: the hum controller + instrument bank + player voice helpers)

Architecture mirrors Helios exactly: `createBeatLevelAudio` + `createScore` + `createArrangement` + `defineInstruments` + the `voice()` spec layer. No cross-level imports; timing constants all come from `timing.ts`.

### Section-by-section arrangement
- injection (bars 0–4): hum + sparse bar-downbeat kick, tick hats, per-beat arp blips rising in velocity, half-bar riser into stage-1.
- stage-1 (4–12): four-on-floor kick, offbeat hats, 8th-note root bass pulse (hypnotic), sparse 16th arp, 4-bar pad swells.
- stage-2 (12–20): + acid resonant-303 16th line, clap on 2/4, busier hat+openHat, bass octave/fifth `bassFigure`, arp up an octave, pad swells.
- interlock (20–28): klaxon (two-tone) + impact at bar 20, hardened grid-locked double-kick, alarm swells every 2 bars, a per-bar rising noise riser building toward the peak, bar-27 accelerating snare roll. The pulse stays locked — no syncopation outside this escalation.
- muzzle (28–32): bar-28 impact + crash + deep duck + `humCut`, then near-silence: long E-major pad bloom (Picardy third), sparse delayed sparkle arp, tiny sub heartbeat fading 0.6→0.12 on downbeats. No drums.

### The climbing hum
`installMassDriverHum(context, mix)` builds a detuned saw pair + sine (all through a lowpass into `mix.music`) with a slow always-on idle LFO. Oscillators run for the whole session; the controller only steers frequency/cutoff/level (`glideTo`, `idle`, `cutAt`), so a cut leaves it ready to re-idle. A single shared `humTrack` fn drives it at every bar downbeat via `humMidiAtBar(bar+1)`: E1→33 (linear to bar 12)→40/E2 (bar 20)→then a t² eased (accelerating) charge 40→52/E3 by bar 27, cut dead at bar 28. Level fades in over the breech and swells through the charge; cutoff tracks pitch so the climb audibly brightens. Trace verified the exact climb bar-by-bar and the cut at 52.56s. In trace mode (no live context) the track records a `hum` event instead of steering nodes. `idle` on runend re-idles; a `died` runend cuts it and drops a low detonation rumble instead of the bloom.

### Kill-lane design
Five authored 32-step lanes (degrees into the live 8-note lead set, so all validate): 0 = sparse glassy climbing arches; 1 = steady locked walk mirroring the four-on-floor; 2 = high jump-cut acid fragments leaving the bass its register; 3 = urgent ceiling-pressing climbs (countdown); 4 = glassy descents resolving onto the E-major tonic. Interlock kills bypass the lane for a bespoke climbing confirmation (root + n ascending steps, louder/brighter/higher each of the 6, + clamp clank + ignition ping); `bossphase destroyed` ducks and lands a conclusive rising figure over an impact.

### Gain-staging decisions
Tuned by perceived loudness (saw ≫ sine): saws/squares kept low (reese 0.08, stab 0.045/voice, pad 0.045, arp 0.06), sines/sub high (kick 0.6, bass sub 0.26, impact 0.5). Rhythmic bass voiced at E2 (sub) so it doesn't fight the hum's E1 drone. Kick duck is the pump — `duckAt(time, 0.5, 0.18)`, grid-locked. Mix bus: compressor (−15/5), delay 3×16th feedback 0.3 damped 2.2kHz, reverb 2.2s, noiseSeconds 2, volumeScale 0.8. Section-4 player voices are glassy/quiet/reverb-heavy per the doctrine.

### Verification
- `tsc` on `levels/mass-driver-wo4m/audio*` → clean (remaining repo errors are in the other agents' `index.ts`/`visuals/environment.ts`).
- `trace:audio` runs clean: 982 events; sections at 0/7.56/22.56/37.56/52.56s (bars 0/4/12/20/28);

## Summary

I replaced the scaffold in `src/levels/mass-driver-wo4m/gameplay.ts` with the full gameplay spine, mirroring Helios's structure and importing all shared timing from `./timing`.

### Key tuning decisions
- **Rail** (`createMassDriverRail`): deterministic 1600-unit bore, straight down −z with a gentle weave (lateral ≤3.3, wavelength ~228u, vertical ≤1.9) that tapers to zero past `MUZZLE_U` and lifts gently into open space; `catmullrom` tension 0.38.
- **Speed profile**: strictly-accelerating piecewise-linear keys (0.50 → 0.62 → 0.85 → 1.08 → 1.32 → 1.42, then a 0.25-beat spike to 4.3 easing to 3.4). `MUZZLE_U = massDriverRunProgress(SHOT_TIME) ≈ 0.63`.
- **Choreography**: wave-builders `coilRank`/`threaderWeave`/`capacitors`/`interlockRank`, all authored via `bar(...)`. 72 counted enemies (coil 29, threader 33, capacitor 4, interlock 6) across bars 1–27; bars 28–32 empty. Teaching waves bars 1–4, 2-bar coil/threader cadence through stage-1 (capacitor at bar 8), density + arc-firing coils + two capacitors in stage-2 with a breath before bar 20, six interlocks as two ranks of three (bars 20/22) with light threader chaff bars 22–27.
- **Interlocks**: I made them **station-keep** rather than sit on a literal fixed anchor — at these rail speeds a true `railAnchor` for a bar-20→bar-28 hold would spawn ~380 units downbore (far past the fog, unkillable). The clamp holds a roughly constant distance ahead of the camera (menacing idle), is never overtaken, and calls `damagePlayer(99)`/returns true at `runTime >= SHOT_TIME`. Verified via the engagement report: all six are lockable from spawn to 51.95–52.25s (10.6–14.7s windows).
- **Coils** ride the bore wall at radius ~10 (design-accurate, and required to push kills off dead-center given the 60° FOV); firing coils use the Helios scorcher rear-back→lunge telegraph. Arcs mirror Helios's bolt exactly (`steerHomingShot`/`updateHostileShotImpact`/`shotBehindCamera`, intercept tracking via fire/kill/miss). Boss protocol emits `bossphase summoned`/`destroyed` off the bus.
- **`timing`**: deliberately inherited the engine default (commented) — at 128 BPM the tempo-adaptive grid-ramp and 32nd-note snap fit the slow barrel cruise.

### Simulate highlights (final)
- **none (no-fire)**: `died at 52.52s` — the interlock detonation at SHOT_TIME (1 player hit = the 99-damage blow). The fail path produces `died: true` cleanly.
- **perfect**: 72/72 kills, score 13959, rank S; only gap 52.0–59.5s (the intended muzzle-exit); pressure peak 5, impossible moments 0; all events fired.
- **imperfect**: survives all four seeds (1–4), rank S, 58–65/72 kills — survives well more often than not.
- **heatmap**: perfect center% 32.4 / avg dist 45.5m; imperfect 5.4% / 32.2m — both pass the fail gates (70% / 60m) and beat the Helios reference (63.3% / 59.4m); kills spread across the frame.
- **engagement**: 0 failed, 72 measured-only — no rail-end or window failures.

### Left for the integrator
- Rank ladder (S at 13000/0.8) is close to the perfect-run score of ~13959; retune from the final perfect score once audio/scoring settle, per the design note.
- Under no-fire the level detonates exactly at bar 28 (ideal), but a player who intercepts arcs yet ignores interlocks would still hit the same detonation path — both fail routes are wired to `died: true`.

Typecheck: my `gameplay.ts` and its timing/index integration are clean (the earlier index.ts errors were the visuals module's missing exports and have since resolved as that agent landed theirs).

All expose `userData.parts` for the tint pass.
- **`effects.ts`** — pooled electric spark shards (instanced), ring pulses, glints, jagged arc-lightning polylines, camera-facing flash disc (muzzle whiteout / detonation core), projectile trails; `resetEffects`/`updateEffects`.

inputs.command: cat scripts/check-level-scope.sh
inputs.description: Read scope check script to understand diff baseline

events.resultText:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

usage() {
  echo "Usage: npm run check:scope -- <level-id> [base-ref]" >&2
}

if [ "$#" -lt 1 ] || [ "$#" -gt 2 ]; then
  usage
  exit 2
fi

level_id=$1
base_ref=${2:-main}
allowed_prefix="src/levels/${level_id}/"
allowed_registry="src/levels/index.ts"
allowed_gallery="docs/level-gallery.md"

if ! git rev-parse --verify --quiet "$base_ref" >/dev/null; then
  echo "Unknown base ref: $base_ref" >&2
  exit 2
fi

changed_files=$(
  {
    git diff --name-only "$base_ref"
    git ls-files --others --exclude-standard
  } | sort -u
)

[ -n "$changed_files" ] || exit 0

out_of_scope=$(printf '%s\n' "$changed_files" | awk -v prefix="$allowed_prefix" -v registry="$allowed_registry" -v gallery="$allowed_gallery" '
  $0 != registry && $0 != gallery && index($0, prefix) != 1 { print }
')

if [ -n "$out_of_scope" ]; then
  echo "Out-of-scope files for level '$level_id':" >&2
  printf '%s\n' "$out_of_scope" >&2
  exit 1
fi

events.text:
**Mass Driver is complete** — a full showcase level at `src/levels/mass-driver-wo4m/`, registered in the picker, with every gate green: `typecheck`, `build`, `check:scope` (against the entrant baseline), `check:floor` (simulation, occlusion 0 warnings, perf, audio config, gallery), and `trace:audio`.

## What was built

**The core conceit is structural, not decorative.** 128 BPM × 32 bars = exactly 60 seconds, and the 112 accelerator rings are placed at `runProgress(beat × beatSeconds)` — so the camera crosses one ring per beat *by construction*, at any point on the strictly-accelerating speed profile. Rings space out in world units as you accelerate but never leave the grid, and each ring heat-shifts arc blue → violet → blinding white across the run.

**The gun is the instrument.** A persistent tonal drone climbs from E1 bar-by-bar, accelerating (t²) through the interlock bars into the firing charge, and cuts dead on the bar-28 shot. Under it: locked four-on-floor minimal techno in E minor (acid line from stage-2, Em–F phrygian dread in the boss), with all player actions transport-quantized and pitched from the live harmony — kills walk hidden per-section melodic lanes, each of the six interlock kills plays a climbing confirmation figure, and rejects are a cold breaker-trip clunk. After the shot, everything strips to a lone **E major** bloom in silent open space — the whole run is minor; the release is the light.

**The boss is a deadline, not a health bar.** Six hazard-amber safety interlocks clamp the bore in two ranks (bars 20/22), station-keeping ~1s ahead so they ring the frame. Clear all six before the charge peaks at bar 28 and the gun fires — whiteout, ~3× speed spike, star-streaked vacuum, "PAYLOAD AWAY". Any interlock alive at bar 28 detonates the barrel (99-damage hit, `died: true`, red containment-failure flash).

**Simulation results:** perfect policy 72/72 kills → S; seeded imperfect 66/72 → A (S requires the gun fired + 95% clear); no-fire dies at exactly 52.52s (the deadline). The only dead-air gap is the intentional post-shot silence.

## My refinements on the agents' output

Fixed an inverted arc-interception counter (it counted *every* kill), retuned the S-rank margin off the sim ceiling, made the detonation zero the hull line, clamped threader corkscrews inside the bore + moved the wall ribs out (occlusion 17 warnings → 0), shortened engagement leads (kill distance 45.8m → 40.1m, wider screen spread), pulled the interlocks from distant specks to frame-ringing clamps, and made the charge glow quadratic + distance-capped so the boss stays readable until the final beats.

## Needs human eyes (WSL2 can't render WebGPU headless)

Verified via snapshots: tunnel arc, bloom-off readability, interlock legibility against the charge, the whiteout, and the starfield exit. A playtester should first check **rings landing on the beat by feel**, the **bar-28 shot moment** (whiteout length, FOV kick), the **charge crescendo brightness** at their bloom setting, the six-segment reticle gauge during live play, and the music mix balance (hum vs. kick duck).
